commonIssues: Root and stem rot from overwatering or pots standing in water (UConn, RHS); always let the upper soil dry before watering
Leaves fail to develop characteristic splits/holes (fenestration) in inadequate light (Wisconsin Horticulture, RHS)
Large leaves sunburn easily when moved into stronger direct sun (UConn)
Brown leaf edges from dry indoor air; prefers medium-to-high humidity (Wisconsin Horticulture, RHS)
Pests: mealybugs, spider mites, scale, aphids, thrips (Wisconsin Horticulture, Missouri Botanical Garden)
Salt burn: easily damaged by high soil salts, so fertilize at reduced strength (UConn)
All parts except fully ripe fruit contain calcium oxalate and are toxic to people and pets (Wisconsin Horticulture, NC State)
Grown indoors as a foliage plant; flowering and fruiting are rare in home environments (Missouri Botanical Garden, NC State)
Top-heavy vine: needs a moss pole, bark slab, or sturdy stake to prevent stem breakage (UConn, Wisconsin Horticulture, Missouri Botanical Garden)

source.citation: University of Connecticut Home & Garden Education Center, 'Monstera deliciosa' factsheet — partial shade with 3-6 hours direct sun daily; allow top 2-3 inches of soil to dry between waterings; hardy zones 10a-12b, tolerates outdoor placement above 45F; fertilize during active growth (May-September), easily burned by high soil salts; well-draining mix with high organic matter; repot gradually, tolerates pot-bound better than over-potting; moss pole support
UConn Home & Garden Education Center — watering: allow top 2-3 in of soil to dry between waterings (roughly weekly indoors); susceptible to root disease in cool months, needs consistent drainage

University of Wisconsin-Madison Extension, Wisconsin Horticulture, 'Swiss-Cheese Plant, Monstera deliciosa' — does not grow below 50F (10C), frost kills; bright light in summer, direct sun in winter; water thoroughly and allow soil to dry slightly between waterings, less in winter; fertilize regularly spring to fall; needs ample root room and support (tree bark or moss-covered pole); pests: aphids, mealybugs, scale, spider mites

Royal Horticultural Society, 'How to grow Swiss cheese plants' — keep at 18-25C year-round; indirect light by an east/west window; let compost nearly dry then water thoroughly, never leave standing in water; feed balanced liquid fertilizer April-September; repot into only slightly larger pots; mix of 1 part ericaceous peat-free compost to 2 parts orchid compost; train onto a moss pole

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder, 'Monstera deliciosa' — USDA zones 10-12; bright indoor light, no strong direct sun; medium water, regular in growing season, reduced fall through late winter; peaty soil-based potting mix; 6-8 ft indoors (30-70 ft in habitat); needs pole/trellis support; indoor plants rarely flower and fruit; pests include aphids, mealybugs, thrips, scale, spider mites

North Carolina State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox, 'Monstera deliciosa' — tolerates dappled sunlight or partial shade (2-6 hours direct sun); water thoroughly then allow top quarter-to-third of soil to dry; zones 10a-12b, houseplant elsewhere; climbing vine 6-8 ft indoors; rarely fruits indoors; toxic (calcium oxalate); medium maintenance, sturdy support required

Confidence note: sources give watering as a soil-dryness test (top 2-3 in dry), not a fixed interval; 7-day base interval is a conservative indoor estimate. Container liters are estimated (10 L = ~25 cm pot for an established plant; sources advise gradual up-potting in only slightly larger pots, no explicit volumes). heatStressC 35C and hotWeatherMultiplier 0.8 are conservative estimates from the 18-29C preferred range; feeding interval 30 days estimated from 'fertilize regularly/monthly during the April-September growing season'.
